story:
The mall is a blur on Thursdays—colors, bags, tourists stuck in the bottleneck of store entrances. I slip through on instinct, boots clicking across tile, grateful for knowing the shortcuts to Dillard’s. Light pours in through the glass ceiling, making the autumn displays glow like a dream: flannel on mannequins, leather boots, jewel-toned shadows, and rows of perfume and cologne. A scent of mahogany and pine hits me. The bright floor and glassy light tilt into mud and rain. I am twelve again.

I’d left the back door open while hauling a bulging bag of trash, rain drowning out Mama’s reminder to shut it tight. Our dachshund, Tucker, saw his chance and bolted. I dropped the trash into the dumpster and tore after him, feet sucking into mud, hair plastered to my face. He veered hard right. I followed—and crashed straight into a cherry-red bicycle and the boy riding it.

We went down in a splash. Tucker danced around the boy’s ankles, yipping. The boy laughed like falling was the best part of his day. “I meant the face you made,” he said when I complained, twisting his mouth into a ridiculous O before doubling over, cackling. He jogged after me as I stalked away, caught my wrist gently, and stuck out a hand. “Callum.”

“Mary,” I muttered, then we shook. He smelled like something woodsy and sweet.

“Cologne?” I asked, skeptical. His ears went pink. “My Papa’s,” he admitted. “He says it makes him brave. Years from now, a salesclerk in Dillard’s will lift a square glass bottle containing it. I thought it might help me learn to ride. That was crash number five.”

He wanted to ride over to see me, he said. He didn’t know how to ride. I didn’t either, not really—but it turned out I was a natural. The bike’s pedals surrendered under my feet, wobbly at first, then steady. Callum groaned that his bicycle liked me better. I coached him anyway, insisting posture was everything. After three more spills and one triumphant five-foot glide, we retreated to his porch swing. His mom handed us lemonade. We sipped and watched the rain bead on the railing. From his yard, my roof looked small and far.

“I was riding to your house when you ran into me,” he confessed later, cheeks pinking again. “We’re moving next week.”

The word moving punched the air from my lungs. Neither of us had phones or email. We sat with the clink of ice and the smell of Empowered Teakwood, bravery bottled. He brightened. We still had a week. We made it count.

We pedaled and stumbled and tried again. We shared lemonade and grilled cheese at each other’s houses, read under trees, played fetch with Tucker, and danced in bursts of summer rain. He never became a champion rider, but he came close. On the last day, we linked pinkies—“the pinkiest”—and promised we’d see each other again. I hugged him long enough to memorize the scent on his shirt. Then the van rolled away down the dirt road, and I waved through stinging eyes. He waved back.

Back in Dillard’s, my heart thumps like I’ve been running. A salesclerk appears at my shoulder, lifting a square glass bottle. “Empowered Teakwood, madam? Very popular. It attracts the ladies, no?” He winks. The words land exactly the way Callum said they would. I laugh softly, the sound light as the memory. “It sure does,” I say, and leave without buying it.

Outside the mall, the air smells like cool weather and possibility. Maybe I’ll ride my bike when I get home.
